nodes.name: Plays & Playwrights
nodes.description: Dramatic works and the writers who created them

items.prompt: This Prince of Denmark feigns madness, delivers the 'To be, or not to be' soliloquy, and avenges his father's murder by killing his uncle Claudius. Name the prince.
items.explanation:
Prince of Denmark; protagonist of Shakespeare's longest play (c. 1600–1601)
His father's ghost reveals that Claudius murdered him by pouring poison in his ear
Feigns madness ('antic disposition') to investigate the murder; stages a play-within-a-play ('The Mousetrap') to confirm Claudius's guilt
Delivers the most famous soliloquy in English literature: 'To be, or not to be'
Accidentally kills Polonius behind a curtain, setting off a chain of deaths
Dies in the final scene from a poisoned sword; Horatio survives to tell his story
'Good night, sweet prince, / And flights of angels sing thee to thy rest' — Horatio's farewell
items.cardFront: *Hamlet* — Prince of Denmark, avenges his father, 'To be or not to be'

items.prompt: In *Hamlet*, this young noblewoman is the daughter of Polonius and the prince's love interest. After her father is killed, she descends into madness, sings strange songs, and drowns in a brook. Name her.
items.explanation:
Daughter of Polonius and sister of Laertes; her relationship with the prince is manipulated by her family and the court
Her father uses her as bait to spy on the prince, who tells her 'Get thee to a nunnery'
After Polonius's death, she loses her sanity and distributes flowers with symbolic meanings (rosemary for remembrance, rue for regret)
Drowns in a brook wreathed in flowers; Gertrude describes it in one of Shakespeare's most lyrical passages (Act 4, Scene 7)
Her death is ambiguous — the gravediggers debate whether it was suicide or accident
John Everett Millais's painting *Ophelia* (1851–1852) is one of the most famous Pre-Raphaelite works
The name has become a literary archetype for tragic, doomed innocence
items.cardFront: *Hamlet* — Polonius's daughter, love interest, drowns amid madness

items.prompt: In *Macbeth*, this ambitious queen goads her husband into murdering King Duncan, then is haunted by guilt and sleepwalks while crying 'Out, damned spot!' Name her.
items.explanation:
Wife of the Scottish thane who becomes king through murder; she is the driving force behind Duncan's assassination
Persuades her husband by questioning his manhood: 'When you durst do it, then you were a man'
Invokes dark spirits: 'Unsex me here, and fill me from the crown to the toe top-full of direst cruelty'
The sleepwalking scene (Act 5, Scene 1) — 'Out, damned spot! Out, I say!' — reveals her psychological collapse
Dies offstage, presumably by suicide; her death prompts the 'Tomorrow and tomorrow and tomorrow' speech
One of Shakespeare's most psychologically complex female roles; Sarah Siddons's 18th-century portrayal became legendary
Has no first name in the play — historically she may be based on Gruoch of Scotland
items.cardFront: *Macbeth* — Queen who drives the murder plot, 'Out, damned spot!'

items.prompt: In *Othello*, this ensign manipulates the Moorish general into believing his wife Desdemona is unfaithful, orchestrating tragedy through lies and a stolen handkerchief. Name the villain.
items.explanation:
Ensign (ancient) to Othello; often called Shakespeare's greatest villain
Motives are deliberately ambiguous — claims he was passed over for promotion in favor of Cassio, hints at jealousy and racism
Uses Desdemona's handkerchief as false evidence of her affair with Cassio
Manipulates every character in the play, including his own wife Emilia, who unknowingly delivers the handkerchief
Samuel Taylor Coleridge described his soliloquies as 'the motive-hunting of motiveless malignity'
Refuses to explain his actions when caught: 'Demand me nothing. What you know, you know. / From this time forth I never will speak word'
The role is considered one of the most demanding villain parts in the canon; actors often play both Othello and the ensign in alternation
items.cardFront: *Othello* — Ensign, master manipulator, stolen handkerchief plot

items.prompt: In *The Tempest*, this exiled Duke of Milan uses magic to shipwreck his enemies on a remote island, commands the spirit Ariel, and ultimately renounces his sorcery. Name the magician.
items.explanation:
Rightful Duke of Milan, overthrown by his brother Antonio and set adrift with his infant daughter Miranda
Spends 12 years on the island mastering magic from his books; commands the airy spirit Ariel and enslaves Caliban
Conjures the tempest that opens the play to bring his enemies within reach
His 'Our revels now are ended' speech (Act 4, Scene 1) is among Shakespeare's most quoted passages
Renounces magic in the final act: 'I'll break my staff... I'll drown my book'
Widely read as Shakespeare's farewell to the stage — *The Tempest* (c. 1610–1611) was likely his last solo-authored play
The name derives from Latin 'prosperus' (fortunate); his opposite, Caliban, is near-anagram of 'cannibal'
items.cardFront: *The Tempest* — Exiled duke, sorcerer, commands Ariel, renounces magic

items.prompt: In *A Midsummer Night's Dream*, this mischievous fairy servant applies a love potion to the wrong Athenian's eyes, causing romantic chaos, and declares 'Lord, what fools these mortals be!' Name the trickster sprite.
items.explanation:
Also called Robin Goodfellow; servant to Oberon, King of the Fairies
A hobgoblin from English folklore long before Shakespeare — associated with pranks, shape-shifting, and leading travelers astray
Mistakenly applies the love-juice to Lysander's eyes instead of Demetrius's, scrambling the romantic pairings
'Lord, what fools these mortals be!' (Act 3, Scene 2) — his most famous line
Speaks the play's epilogue: 'If we shadows have offended, / Think but this, and all is mended'
Transforms Bottom the weaver's head into a donkey's head, causing Titania (under her own love spell) to fall for him
One of Shakespeare's most beloved comic characters; the role is often doubled with Philostrate or Oberon in modern productions
items.cardFront: *A Midsummer Night's Dream* — Trickster fairy, love-potion mix-up, 'What fools these mortals be'

items.prompt: In *The Merchant of Venice*, this wealthy heiress of Belmont disguises herself as a male lawyer and delivers the 'quality of mercy' speech to defeat the moneylender's claim in court. Name her.
items.explanation:
Heiress of Belmont; bound by her dead father's will to marry whoever chooses the correct casket (lead, silver, or gold)
Bassanio chooses the lead casket ('Who chooseth me must give and hazard all he hath') and wins her hand
Disguises herself as the lawyer Balthasar to defend Antonio in court against Shylock's bond
Delivers 'The quality of mercy is not strained' speech (Act 4, Scene 1) — one of Shakespeare's most famous orations
Defeats Shylock on a technicality: the bond specifies a pound of flesh but no blood, making it impossible to collect
Speaks over 20% of the play's lines — the largest female role in Shakespeare by word count
Also engineers the ring-test subplot, tricking Bassanio into giving away the ring she gave him
items.cardFront: *The Merchant of Venice* — Heiress, disguised lawyer, 'quality of mercy' speech

items.prompt: This 13-year-old Capulet daughter speaks from her balcony, asking 'Wherefore art thou Romeo?' and takes a sleeping potion to fake her death, leading to double tragedy. Name her.
items.explanation:
Daughter of Lord and Lady Capulet; just 13 years old (her father notes she 'hath not seen the change of fourteen years')
'Wherefore art thou Romeo?' means 'Why are you Romeo?' — lamenting his Montague name, not asking where he is
Secretly marries Romeo with Friar Laurence's help; the marriage lasts less than a day before Romeo is banished
Takes Friar Laurence's sleeping potion to fake her death and escape a forced marriage to Count Paris
Wakes in the Capulet tomb to find Romeo truly dead from poison; kills herself with his dagger
The play (c. 1594–1596) draws from Arthur Brooke's poem *The Tragicall Historye of Romeus and Juliet* (1562)
The balcony scene (Act 2, Scene 2) is the most iconic love scene in Western literature
items.cardFront: *Romeo and Juliet* — Capulet daughter, balcony scene, 'Wherefore art thou Romeo?'

items.prompt: In *The Merchant of Venice*, this Jewish moneylender demands a 'pound of flesh' from Antonio as forfeit on a loan, and asks 'If you prick us, do we not bleed?' Name him.
items.explanation:
Jewish moneylender in Venice; lends 3,000 ducats to Bassanio with Antonio's flesh as bond
'If you prick us, do we not bleed?' (Act 3, Scene 1) — his plea for common humanity is one of Shakespeare's most debated speeches
Insists on his 'pound of flesh' in court but is defeated when Portia (disguised as Balthasar) rules he may take flesh but not shed blood
Forced to convert to Christianity and forfeit half his wealth as punishment — a resolution modern audiences find deeply troubling
The character's portrayal oscillates between antisemitic caricature and sympathetic outsider, depending on production choices
The play (c. 1596–1599) was written during a period when Jews were officially expelled from England (since 1290)
Al Pacino played him in the 2004 film; Patrick Stewart in a 2011 production set the play in Las Vegas
items.cardFront: *The Merchant of Venice* — Moneylender, 'pound of flesh', 'do we not bleed?'

items.prompt: In *A Midsummer Night's Dream*, this ruler of the fairy realm quarrels with his queen over a changeling boy and orders his servant to use a magic flower's juice to teach her a lesson. Name the fairy king.
items.explanation:
King of the Fairies in *A Midsummer Night's Dream* (c. 1595–1596)
His quarrel with Titania over an Indian changeling boy causes storms and disorder in the natural world
Orders Puck to fetch the flower 'love-in-idleness' (a pansy touched by Cupid's arrow) whose juice causes love at first sight
Uses the juice on Titania so she falls in love with Bottom, who has been given a donkey's head
Also orders Puck to apply the juice to the Athenian lovers' eyes, leading to comic mix-ups
The name Oberon derives from Auberon in the medieval French romance *Huon of Bordeaux*
Often doubled with the role of Theseus in modern productions, reflecting the fairy/mortal world parallel
items.cardFront: *A Midsummer Night's Dream* — Fairy king, quarrels with queen, magic love-juice

items.prompt: In *A Midsummer Night's Dream*, this fairy queen quarrels with her husband over a changeling boy and, enchanted by a love potion, falls in love with a weaver who has been given a donkey's head. Name her.
items.explanation:
Queen of the Fairies; her quarrel with Oberon over an Indian changeling boy drives the play's supernatural plot
Shakespeare took the name from Ovid's *Metamorphoses*, where it is an epithet for Diana (the goddess)
Enchanted by the love-in-idleness flower, she dotes on Bottom the weaver, who has been transformed with an ass's head
Her attendant fairies — Peaseblossom, Cobweb, Moth, and Mustardseed — minister to Bottom at her command
The disruption of nature caused by the fairy quarrel (floods, failed harvests, sick seasons) reflects Elizabethan beliefs about cosmic order
Reconciles with Oberon after he removes the spell; they bless the marriages of the mortals in the final act
Often doubled with the role of Hippolyta in modern productions
items.cardFront: *A Midsummer Night's Dream* — Fairy queen, enchanted to love a donkey-headed weaver

items.prompt: In *The Tempest*, this half-human creature is the son of the witch Sycorax and was the island's sole inhabitant before being enslaved by the exiled duke. He plots rebellion and delivers the haunting 'isle is full of noises' speech. Name him.
items.explanation:
Son of the Algerian witch Sycorax; the island's only native inhabitant before Prospero's arrival
Enslaved by Prospero after allegedly attempting to assault Miranda; forced to carry wood and do menial labor
His name is likely a near-anagram of 'cannibal' — derived from the Carib people, via Montaigne's essay 'Of Cannibals'
'Be not afeard; the isle is full of noises, / Sounds and sweet airs, that give delight and hurt not' (Act 3, Scene 2) — one of the play's most beautiful passages
Plots to overthrow Prospero with the drunken Stephano and Trinculo, offering to serve Stephano as his new master
Widely interpreted as a symbol of colonized peoples — Prospero teaches him language, then uses it to command him
The character has been reclaimed in postcolonial literature, notably in Aimé Césaire's *Une Tempête* (1969)
items.cardFront: *The Tempest* — Son of Sycorax, enslaved native, 'isle is full of noises'

items.prompt: In *Twelfth Night*, this shipwrecked young woman disguises herself as a male page called Cesario, enters the service of Duke Orsino, and creates a love triangle when her mistress Olivia falls for the disguise. Name her.
items.explanation:
Shipwrecked on the coast of Illyria; believes her twin brother Sebastian is dead (he survived separately)
Disguises herself as the male page Cesario and enters the service of Duke Orsino, with whom she falls in love
Sent by Orsino to woo the Countess Olivia on his behalf — but Olivia falls in love with 'Cesario' instead
The resulting love triangle (Viola loves Orsino, Orsino loves Olivia, Olivia loves 'Cesario') drives the comedy
The confusion doubles when Sebastian arrives — Olivia mistakes him for Cesario and marries him
Resolved when the twins appear together; Orsino realizes Viola's devotion and proposes to her
The cross-dressing role was originally played by a boy actor pretending to be a woman pretending to be a man
items.cardFront: *Twelfth Night* — Shipwrecked woman, disguised as Cesario, serves Duke Orsino

items.prompt: In *Twelfth Night*, this pompous steward of Olivia's household is tricked by a forged letter into wearing yellow cross-gartered stockings and smiling foolishly, believing his mistress loves him. Name the steward.
items.explanation:
Steward to the Countess Olivia; his name means 'ill will' in Italian
Puritanical and self-important; clashes with Sir Toby Belch, Sir Andrew Aguecheek, and Maria over their late-night reveling
Maria forges a letter in Olivia's handwriting containing 'M.O.A.I.' — he convinces himself the letters refer to him
The letter instructs him to wear yellow cross-gartered stockings and smile constantly — things Olivia despises
Locked in a dark room as a supposed madman; Feste the Fool visits him disguised as 'Sir Topas' the curate
His final line — 'I'll be revenged on the whole pack of you!' — strikes a discordant note against the play's comic resolution
'Some are born great, some achieve greatness, and some have greatness thrust upon 'em' — from the forged letter, often misattributed as Shakespeare's own philosophy
items.cardFront: *Twelfth Night* — Pompous steward, tricked by forged letter, yellow stockings

items.prompt: In the history play bearing his name, this hunchbacked Yorkist king schemes his way to the English throne through murder, then loses the Battle of Bosworth Field, crying 'A horse, a horse! My kingdom for a horse!' Name the king.
items.explanation:
Last Plantagenet king of England (reigned 1483–1485); killed at the Battle of Bosworth Field by Henry Tudor's forces
Shakespeare depicts him as a deformed, Machiavellian schemer — largely Tudor propaganda to legitimize Henry VII's claim
Opens the play with the famous soliloquy: 'Now is the winter of our discontent / Made glorious summer by this sun of York'
Orders the murder of the young princes in the Tower (Edward V and Richard of York) — one of history's enduring mysteries
'A horse, a horse! My kingdom for a horse!' (Act 5, Scene 4) — his desperate cry at Bosworth as his army collapses
His skeleton was discovered under a Leicester car park in 2012; reburied at Leicester Cathedral in 2015
The role is one of the longest in the Shakespeare canon and a legendary star vehicle — Laurence Olivier's 1955 film is definitive
items.cardFront: *Richard III* — Yorkist king, schemes to the throne, 'A horse! My kingdom for a horse!'
